title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: seqcomp compares two sequential probabilistic forecasters. basic question : outcomes arrive time, one forecaster consistently scoring better ? package follows convention scores positively oriented: larger scores better. two forecasters p q, pointwise score difference δ̂t=S(pt,yt)−S(qt,yt). \hat{\delta}_t = S(p_t, y_t) - S(q_t, y_t). Positive values favour forecaster p; negative values favour forecaster q.

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: Suppose observe sequence binary outcomes. Now create two forecasters. Forecaster p mildly informative: tends assign higher probability event occurs lower probability . Forecaster q less informative stays closer 0.5. toy example deliberately simple. real application, p q come two forecasting models, analysts, institutions.

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: easiest workflow use compare_forecasts(). output contains one row per time point. important columns : score_p: score forecaster p, score_q: score forecaster q, delta: score difference score_p - score_q, estimate: running mean score difference, lower upper: confidence sequence, e_pq e_qp: two one-sided e-processes.

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: confidence sequence tracks running average score advantage.  whole interval lies zero, data favour p. whole interval lies zero, data favour q. interval still contains zero, comparison yet decisive chosen level.
code: plot(   cmp$t, cmp$estimate,   type = "l",   ylim = range(c(cmp$lower, cmp$upper, 0), finite = TRUE),   xlab = "Time",   ylab = "Mean score difference",   main = "Sequential comparison using the Brier score" ) lines(cmp$t, cmp$lower, lty = 2) lines(cmp$t, cmp$upper, lty = 2) abline(h = 0, col = "gray50")

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: e-process evidence process. Larger values mean stronger evidence corresponding null hypothesis. two-sided comparison level alpha, rejection threshold used eprocess() 2 / alpha. column e_pq gives evidence p outperforms q. column e_qp gives evidence q outperforms p.  can summarize rejection times eprocess_rejections().
code: alpha <- 0.05 threshold <- 2 / alpha  threshold #> [1] 40 plot(   cmp$t, cmp$e_pq,   type = "l",   log = "y",   xlab = "Time",   ylab = "e-process value",   main = "Evidence that p outperforms q" ) abline(h = threshold, lty = 2, col = "gray50") eprocess_rejections(cmp, alpha = alpha) #> $threshold #> [1] 40 #>  #> $tau_pq #> [1] 79 #>  #> $tau_qp #> [1] NA #>  #> $reject_pq #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $reject_qp #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: wrapper convenient, package also exposes individual pieces. First compute scores. construct confidence sequence. construct e-process. gives core information compare_forecasts(), manual control.

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: binary probability forecasts, "brier" safest starting point. spherical score also bounded can used finite-sample confidence sequences e-processes. logarithmic score unbounded. Therefore compare_forecasts() uses asymptotic confidence sequence default compute e-process. binary log-score comparisons Winkler construction appropriate, use winkler_compare().

title: Getting started with seqcomp
text: Use compare_forecasts() common workflow. Use lower-level functions want control scoring rule, confidence sequence, e-process, lag handling, predictable bounds. bounded binary categorical scores Brier spherical scores, finite-sample confidence sequences e-processes available. unbounded scores log score, QLIKE, tick loss, CRPS, careful. Use asymptotic confidence sequences, Winkler scores applicable, problem-specific predictable bounds.

title: seqcomp
text: seqcomp R package anytime-valid sequential comparison probabilistic forecasters, implementing framework Choe Ramdas (2024). Given two competing forecasters sequence binary categorical outcomes, package constructs confidence sequences e-processes running mean score difference valid simultaneously every point time, without requiring pre-specified sample size adjustment repeated monitoring. package provides: Positively oriented proper scoring rules (Brier, logarithmic, spherical, CRPS, tick loss, QLIKE, Winkler). Anytime-valid confidence sequences: Hoeffding-style (Theorem 1, Choe Ramdas 2024) empirical Bernstein (Theorem 2). asymptotic confidence sequence unbounded scoring rules (Appendix C, Choe Ramdas 2024). Sub-exponential e-processes sequential hypothesis testing (Theorem 3). Winkler-score tools one-sided sequential inference unbounded binary scoring rules. Lag-handling utilities multi-step-ahead forecast evaluation via stream splitting. Predictable-bounds tools settings score differences globally bounded advance. boundary computations (normal mixture, gamma-exponential mixture, polynomial stitching) implemented scratch, dependency confseq package.

title: seqcomp
text: Available CRAN: development version can installed GitHub:

title: seqcomp
text: compare_forecasts() main entry point. computes pointwise scores, running mean score difference, confidence sequence, two one-sided e-processes single call. column estimate running mean score difference Δ̂t=1t∑=1t(S(pi,yi)−S(qi,yi))\hat{\Delta}_t = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{=1}^{t}(S(p_i,y_i)-S(q_i,y_i)). Positive values favour p; negative values favour q. columns lower upper empirical Bernstein confidence sequence bounds. columns e_pq e_qp two one-sided e-process values; two-sided rejection threshold level alpha = 0.05 2 / 0.05 = 40.
code: library(seqcomp)  set.seed(1) n <- 300 y <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 0.55)  # Forecaster p has some signal; forecaster q always predicts 0.5 p <- ifelse(y == 1, 0.62, 0.38) q <- rep(0.50, n)  out <- compare_forecasts(   p            = p,   q            = q,   y            = y,   scoring_rule = "brier" )  tail(out[, c("t", "estimate", "lower", "upper", "e_pq", "e_qp")]) #>       t estimate      lower     upper    e_pq         e_qp #> 295 295   0.1056 0.07129320 0.1399068 2681618 2.220446e-16 #> 296 296   0.1056 0.07140910 0.1397909 2824574 2.220446e-16 #> 297 297   0.1056 0.07152422 0.1396758 2975161 2.220446e-16 #> 298 298   0.1056 0.07163857 0.1395614 3133784 2.220446e-16 #> 299 299   0.1056 0.07175215 0.1394478 3300873 2.220446e-16 #> 300 300   0.1056 0.07186498 0.1393350 3476882 2.220446e-16 plot(   out$t, out$estimate,   type = "l",   ylim = range(c(out$lower, out$upper, 0), finite = TRUE),   xlab = "Time",   ylab = "Running mean score difference" ) lines(out$t, out$lower, lty = 2, col = "steelblue") lines(out$t, out$upper, lty = 2, col = "steelblue") abline(h = 0, col = "gray50") legend(   "topleft",   legend = c("Estimate", "95% EB confidence sequence"),   lty    = c(1, 2),   col    = c("black", "steelblue"),   bty    = "n" )

title: seqcomp
text: Two scale conventions used throughout, following Choe Ramdas (2024) exactly. Theorem 1 (Hoeffding CS) requires |δ̂|≤c|\hat{\delta}_i| \leq c, c = 1 used Brier spherical score differences [−1,1][-1, 1]. Theorems 2 3 (empirical Bernstein CS e-process) require |δ̂|≤c/2|\hat{\delta}_i| \leq c/2, c = 2 used score differences. compare_forecasts() applies conventions automatically. differ Python comparecast package, applies Theorem 2/3 convention throughout.

title: seqcomp
text: compare_forecasts() convenience wrapper. underlying functions can called directly finer control:
code: scores_p <- brier_score(p, y) scores_q <- brier_score(q, y)  cs  <- cs_bernstein(scores_p, scores_q, alpha = 0.05, c = 2) ep  <- eprocess(scores_p, scores_q, alpha = 0.05, c = 2)

title: seqcomp
text: statistical methods seqcomp based : Confidence sequences time-uniform probability bounds (Howard, Ramdas, McAuliffe Sekhon, 2021). E-values game-theoretic statistics (Ramdas, Grünwald, Vovk Shafer, 2023). Sequential forecaster comparison weak null hypothesis (Choe Ramdas, 2024). package developed part bachelor’s thesis Vienna University Economics Business (WU Vienna).

Time-uniform Chernoff bounds via nonnegative supermartingales. Probability Surveys, 17, 257–317. https://doi.org/10.1214/18-PS321 Ramdas, ., Grünwald, P., Vovk, V. Shafer, G. (2023). what: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
text: Computes positively oriented Brier/quadratic score. Vector probability input treated binary; matrix probability input treated categorical.

title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
text: p Numeric vector [0, 1] binary forecasts, numeric matrix whose rows probability vectors categorical forecasts. y binary vector input, numeric vector {0, 1}. categorical matrix input, integer vector {1, ..., K}, K = ncol(p).

title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
text: Numeric vector scores [-1, 0]. Higher better.

title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
text: binary forecasts, computes $$S(p, y) = -(p-y)^2.$$ categorical forecasts, computes $$S(\mathbf{p}, y) = -\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{p} - e_y\|_2^2,$$ e_y one-hot vector realised category. convention category 2 corresponds binary event y = 1, categorical formula recovers binary formula exactly K = 2.

title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
text: Score differences lie [-1, 1], use c = 1 Theorem 1 c = 2 Theorems 2 3.

title: Brier score for binary and categorical forecasts — brier_score
P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
text: Converts anytime-valid p-values e-values using mixture simple calibrator, used CR23 Section 4.4.

title: P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
text: p Numeric vector p-values (0, 1]. strategy Character. "mixture" (default, Vovk & Wang 2021) "simple". See Details formulas. eps Numeric. Numerical guard log(0). Default: 1e-16.

title: P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
text: Numeric vector e-values >= 0.

title: P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
text: Mixture calibrator (default, matches Python comparecast behaviour): $$f(p) = \frac{1 - p + p\log(p)}{p\,(\log p)^2}$$ Simple calibrator (strategy = "simple"): $$f(p) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{p}}$$

title: P-to-e calibrator — calibrate_p_to_e
Normal mixture (CM) boundary — cm_boundary
text: Normal mixture (CM) boundary

title: Normal mixture (CM) boundary — cm_boundary
Normal mixture (CM) boundary — cm_boundary
text: v Numeric vector >= 0. Intrinsic time values (V_t t). alpha Numeric (0,1). ONE-SIDED significance level. rho Numeric > 0. Tuning parameter. Obtain via rho_from_vopt().

title: Normal mixture (CM) boundary — cm_boundary
text: Numeric vector boundary values (cumulative-sum scale, /t).

title: Normal mixture (CM) boundary — cm_boundary
code: rho <- rho_from_vopt(v_opt = 10, alpha = 0.025)   u   <- cm_boundary(v = 1:500, alpha = 0.025, rho = rho)

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
text: Computes pointwise scores two probabilistic forecasters compares sequentially using confidence sequences , valid finite-sample bounds available, e-processes.

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
code: compare_forecasts(   p,   q,   y,   scoring_rule = c("brier", "spherical", "log"),   alpha = 0.05,   cs_type = NULL,   compute_cs = TRUE,   compute_e = TRUE,   v_opt = 10,   boundary = "mixture",   lcb_only = FALSE,   ucb_only = FALSE,   eps = 1e-15,   clip_max = 1e+07 )

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
text: p Forecasts forecaster 1. binary outcomes, numeric vector probabilities event y = 1. categorical outcomes, numeric matrix whose rows probability vectors. q Forecasts forecaster 2, format p. y Outcomes. binary vector forecasts, numeric vector {0, 1}. categorical matrix forecasts, integer class labels {1, ..., K}. scoring_rule Character. Scoring rule used compare forecasts. Currently supports "brier", "spherical", "log". alpha Numeric (0, 1). Significance level. Default 0.05. cs_type Character NULL. Confidence sequence type: "bernstein", "hoeffding", "asymptotic", "none". NULL, wrapper uses "bernstein" bounded scoring rules ("brier" "spherical") "asymptotic" "log". compute_cs Logical. TRUE, compute confidence sequence. Default TRUE. compute_e Logical. TRUE, compute two one-sided e-processes. Default TRUE. allowed bounded score differences current wrapper, namely "brier" "spherical". v_opt Numeric > 0. Intrinsic time mixture boundary e-process tuned tightest. Default 10. boundary Character. Boundary type passed cs_hoeffding() cs_bernstein(). Default "mixture". lcb_only Logical. TRUE, compute lower one-sided empirical Bernstein CS. used cs_type = "bernstein". ucb_only Logical. TRUE, compute upper one-sided empirical Bernstein CS. used cs_type = "bernstein". eps Numeric. Probability floor passed log_score() scoring_rule = "log". Default 1e-15. clip_max Numeric. Maximum e-process value clipping. Passed eprocess(). Default 1e7.

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
text: data.frame one row per time point columns: t Time index. score_p Pointwise score forecaster p. score_q Pointwise score forecaster q. delta Pointwise score difference, score_p - score_q. estimate Running mean score difference. Positive values favour forecaster p; negative values favour forecaster q. lower, upper Confidence sequence bounds. NA compute_cs = FALSE cs_type = "none". e_pq, e_qp One-sided e-processes. e_pq tests whether forecaster p outperforms q; e_qp tests reverse direction. NA compute_e = FALSE.

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
text: convenience wrapper around brier_score(), spherical_score(), log_score(), cs_hoeffding(), cs_bernstein(), cs_asymptotic(), eprocess(). designed common workflow user two forecast streams p q, outcome stream y, wants single tidy output object. scoring rules seqcomp positively oriented: higher scores better. Therefore $$\hat{\delta}_t = S(p_t, y_t) - S(q_t, y_t)$$ positive forecaster p performs better forecaster q time t. "brier" "spherical", score differences bounded [-1, 1]. wrapper therefore uses c = 1 Hoeffding-style confidence sequences c = 2 empirical Bernstein confidence sequences e-processes. "log", score differences unbounded. wrapper therefore defaults cs_asymptotic() refuses compute finite-sample e-processes. binary log-score comparisons Winkler construction appropriate, use winkler_compare() instead.

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts
text: confidence sequence estimates running average score advantage p q. whole interval lies zero, data favour p; whole interval lies zero, data favour q. e-processes evidence processes one-sided null hypotheses. level alpha, two-sided rejection threshold used eprocess() 2 / alpha.

title: Compare Two Sequential Forecasters — compare_forecasts

what: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
title: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
text: Wrapper scoringRules::crps_sample using method = "edf" (empirical distribution function, O(n log n) via quantile decomposition Laio & Tamea, 2007). Positively oriented: higher = better.

title: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
text: ensemble Matrix. T x n matrix forecast draws. row corresponds one observation y comprises n simulation draws predictive distribution. Historical Simulation: row past WINDOW returns. y Numeric vector length T. Realised observations.

title: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
text: Numeric vector length T CRPS values (-Inf, 0] (negated loss).

title: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
text: Requires nrow(ensemble) == length(y). Passes dat = ensemble directly crps_sample handles vectorisation rows natively. show_messages suppressed "edf" method requires bandwidth selection messages.

title: Negated CRPS for empirical predictive distributions — crps_empirical
code: if (requireNamespace("scoringRules", quietly = TRUE)) {   ensemble <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 1.0, 1.1, 1.2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)   crps_empirical(ensemble, y = c(0.25, 1.05)) } #> [1] -0.03888889 -0.03888889

what: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
title: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
text: Computes Continuous Ranked Probability Score normal predictive distribution using scoringRules::crps_norm() negates higher values better.

title: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
text: mu Numeric vector. Location parameters (conditional means). sigma Numeric vector. Scale parameters (conditional SDs, > 0). x Numeric vector. Realised observations.

title: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
text: Numeric vector CRPS values (-Inf, 0] (negated loss).

title: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
text: Calls scoringRules::crps_norm(y = x, mean = mu, sd = sigma) negates. Use GARCH(1,1)-norm forecasts mu conditional mean sigma conditional standard deviation.

title: Negated CRPS for normal predictive distributions — crps_normal
code: if (requireNamespace("scoringRules", quietly = TRUE)) {   crps_normal(mu = c(0, 1), sigma = c(1, 2), x = c(0.2, 1.3)) } #> [1] -0.2495997 -0.4853088

what: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
title: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
text: Wrapper scoringRules::crps_t. Positively oriented: higher = better. dof > 2 constraint ensures finite variance, required CRPS well-defined t-distribution.

title: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
text: mu Numeric vector. Location parameters (conditional means). sigma Numeric vector. Scale parameters (conditional SDs, > 0). dof Numeric vector scalar. Degrees freedom (> 2). May scalar constant across observations (e.g. estimated per rolling window). x Numeric vector. Realised observations.

title: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
text: Numeric vector CRPS values (-Inf, 0] (negated loss).

title: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
text: Calls scoringRules::crps_t(y = x, df = dof, location = mu,   scale = sigma) negates. Use GARCH(1,1)-std forecasts dof estimated degrees--freedom parameter ugarchroll.

title: Negated CRPS for Student-t predictive distributions — crps_std
code: if (requireNamespace("scoringRules", quietly = TRUE)) {   crps_std(mu = c(0, 1), sigma = c(1, 2), dof = 5, x = c(0.2, 1.3)) } #> [1] -0.2721493 -0.5310947

what: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
title: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
text: Asymptotic, finite-sample: coverage >= 1 - alpha holds t -> infinity. Valid without requiring bounded score differences — requires hat_delta_t finite variance — appropriate tick loss scoring rules hard bounds depend unbounded realised values. Suitable large evaluation windows.

title: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 2. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. Default: 0.05. t_star Numeric > 0. Sample size CS tightest. Default: length(scores1) (tightest end sample).

title: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
text: data.frame columns t, estimate, lower, upper.

title: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic
text: $$C_t^= \hat\Delta_t \pm \sqrt{   \frac{2(t \sigma^2_t \rho^2 + 1)}{t^2 \rho^2}   \log\frac{\sqrt{t \sigma^2_t \rho^2 + 1}}{\alpha}}$$ \(\sigma^2_t = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{=1}^t (\hat\delta_i - \hat\Delta_{-1})^2\) rho tuned tightest t_star: $$\rho(t_{star}) = \sqrt{\frac{2\log(1/\alpha) + \log(1 + 2\log(1/\alpha))}{t_{star}}}$$ running variance estimator uses predictable mean hat_Delta_{t-1} (current mean hat_Delta_t) maintain predictability, hat_Delta_0 := 0.

title: Asymptotic confidence sequence (Appendix C, Eq. 55, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_asymptotic

what: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
text: Constructs variance-adaptive time-uniform CS using empirical intrinsic time \(\hat{V}_t = \sum_{=1}^t (\hat{\delta}_i - \gamma_i)^2\). Tighter Hoeffding CS score differences low variance.

title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
code: cs_bernstein(   scores1,   scores2,   alpha = 0.05,   c = 2,   v_opt = 10,   boundary = "mixture",   gammas = NULL,   lcb_only = FALSE,   ucb_only = FALSE )

title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 2. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. Default: 0.05. c Numeric > 0. Sub-exponential scale. process must satisfy |hat_delta_i| <= c/2. score differences [-b, b-], c = b - (e.g. c = 2 Brier score differences [-1,1]). Default: 2. v_opt Numeric > 0. Optimal intrinsic time. Default: 10. boundary Character. "mixture" (default, GE mixture) "stitching" (polynomial stitched) "hardcoded" (CR23 example formula, valid alpha=0.05, c=1). gammas Numeric vector NULL. Predictable centering sequence. NULL, constructed lagged running mean (default). lcb_only Logical. TRUE, return lower CS : [lower, +Inf). Requires finite lower bound hat_delta_i; provide c. ucb_only Logical. TRUE, return upper CS : (-Inf, upper].

title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
text: data.frame columns t, estimate, lower, upper. lower = -Inf ucb_only = TRUE; upper = Inf lcb_only = TRUE.

title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein
text: CS : $$C_t^{EB} = \hat{\Delta}_t \pm u_{\alpha/2}^{GE}(\hat{V}_t;\, \rho, c) \;/\; t$$

title: Empirical Bernstein confidence sequence (Theorem 2, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_bernstein

what: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: Constructs time-uniform confidence sequence mean score difference \(\Delta_t = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{=1}^t E[\hat{\delta_i} \mid \mathcal{F}_{-1}]\).

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
code: cs_hoeffding(   scores1,   scores2,   alpha = 0.05,   c = 1,   v_opt = 10,   boundary = "mixture" )

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores S(p_t, y_t) forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores S(q_t, y_t) forecaster 2. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. CS coverage 1 - alpha uniformly t. Default: 0.05. c Numeric > 0. Sub-Gaussian scale. process must satisfy |hat_delta_i| <= c . scores [,b], difference [-b, b-], c = b - . Default: 1 (appropriate Brier score differences [-1,1]). v_opt Numeric > 0. Intrinsic time CS tightest. Default: 10 (recommended CR23). boundary Character. "mixture" (default, recommended) "stitching".

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: data.frame columns t, estimate, lower, upper.

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: Requires hat_delta_i c-sub-Gaussian given \(\mathcal{F}_{-1}\), .e. |hat_delta_i| <= c .

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: $$C_t^H = \hat\Delta_t \pm u^{CM}_{\alpha/2}(c^2 t; \rho) / t$$ \(u^{CM}\) normal mixture boundary \(c^2 t\) intrinsic time c-sub-Gaussian process deterministic variance proxy. intrinsic time Theorem 1 v_t = c^2 * t, v_t = t: CM boundary implicitly assumes 1-sub-Gaussian inputs, c^2 scaling must applied explicitly. matches H21 convention, boundary absorbs sub-Gaussian parameter via variance process definition. Relation Python comparecast: Python uses v_t = sigma * t sigma = (hi - lo)/2 = c. equivalent c^2 * t c = 1. c != 1 parametrisations differ; follow paper.

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding
text: Returns data.frame one row per t columns t, estimate (running mean hat_Delta_t), lower, upper, coverage guarantee \(P(\forall t \geq 1 : \Delta_t \[\text{lower}_t, \text{upper}_t]) \geq 1 - \alpha\).

title: Hoeffding-style confidence sequence (Theorem 1, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — cs_hoeffding

what: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
text: Constructs two simultaneous one-sided e-processes sequentially testing whether forecaster 1 (p) outperforms forecaster 2 (q) vice versa.

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
code: eprocess(   scores1,   scores2,   alpha = 0.05,   c = 2,   v_opt = 10,   alpha_opt = NULL,   gammas = NULL,   clip_max = 1e+07 )

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores S(p_t, y_t) forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores S(q_t, y_t) forecaster 2. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. Rejection threshold 2/alpha two-sided test. Default: 0.05. c Numeric > 0. Sub-exponential scale. Must satisfy |hat_delta_i| <= c/2 . score differences [-(b-), b-]: c = 2*(b-). Default: 2 (Brier score differences [-1,1]). v_opt Numeric > 0. Intrinsic time e-process grows fastest. Default: 10 (recommended CR23). alpha_opt Numeric (0,1). One-sided alpha used compute rho. Default: alpha/2 (matches comparecast two-sided convention). gammas Numeric vector NULL. Predictable centering sequence. NULL, constructed lagged running mean. clip_max Numeric. Maximum e-process value clipping. Default: 1e7 (matches Python comparecast).

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
text: data.frame columns t, e_pq, e_qp, log_e_pq, log_e_qp.

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
text: mixture e-process time t : $$E_t^{\mathrm{mix}} = m(S_t, \hat{V}_t)$$ \(S_t = \sum_{=1}^t \hat{\delta}_i\), \(\hat{V}_t = \sum_{=1}^t (\hat{\delta}_i - \gamma_i)^2\), \(m(s, v)\) Gamma-Exponential mixture function (Proposition 3, CR23). VARIANCE PROCESS: intrinsic time V_hat_t uses floor (unlike EB CS). GE mixture m(s, v) well-defined v=0 (returns 1 s=0), floor needed. Adding floor distort e-values. SCALE CONVENTION: c sub-exponential scale parameter |hat_delta_i| <= c/2. Theorems 2 & 3 convention CR23. Brier score differences [-1,1]: c = 2. Winkler scores (bounded 1): c = 2. LOG-SPACE: E-process values computed log-space clipped exponentiating avoid numerical overflow.

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess
text: level alpha: reject \(H_0^w(p, q)\) (conclude p outperforms q) e_pq >= 2/alpha; reject \(H_0^w(q, p)\) (conclude q outperforms p) e_qp >= 2/alpha. Use eprocess_rejections() extract first crossing time .

title: Sub-exponential mixture e-process (Theorem 3, Choe & Ramdas 2023) — eprocess

what: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
text: h-step-ahead forecasts, constructs anytime-valid e-process stream splitting combining h individual e-processes.

title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
code: eprocess_lag(   scores1,   scores2,   h = 1,   alpha = 0.05,   c = 2,   v_opt = 10,   null = "pw",   calibrate = TRUE,   cal_strategy = "mixture" )

title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 2. h Integer >= 1. Forecast lag. h=1, reduces standard eprocess() — splitting. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. Default: 0.05. c Numeric > 0. Sub-exponential scale. Default: 2. v_opt Numeric > 0. Default: 10. null Character. Null hypothesis type: "pw" — period-wise weak null (average combination). "w" — standard weak null (minimum combination). calibrate Logical. Apply p--e calibration. Default: TRUE. cal_strategy Character. "mixture" (default) "simple".

title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
text: data.frame columns t, e_pq, e_qp, log_e_pq, log_e_qp.

title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag
text: h = 1: calls eprocess() directly returns output unchanged. h >= 2: 1. Split xs h streams 2. Compute e-process stream independently 3. Combine using appropriate null rule 4. Convert p-process, combine, calibrate back e-process 5. Unroll original time scale period-wise ("pw") null less conservative standard ("w") null tests different (weaker) hypothesis. See CR23 Section 4.4.

title: Lag-h e-process for sequential forecast comparison (Propositions 5 & 6) — eprocess_lag

what: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
text: Constructs valid e-process score difference bounds vary time predictable (known time -1 observing hat_delta_i).

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
code: eprocess_predictable(   scores1,   scores2,   c_seq,   lambda = NULL,   alpha = 0.05,   gammas = NULL,   clip_max = 1e+07,   strict = FALSE )

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
text: scores1 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 1. scores2 Numeric vector. Scores forecaster 2. c_seq Numeric vector. Predictable bound sequence (c_i), length scores1. Must satisfy |scores1[]-scores2[]| <= c_i/2 c_i > 0 . lambda Numeric [0, 1/c_0). Betting parameter. Must strictly less 1/c_0 c_0 = max(c_seq). NULL, uses recommended default lambda = 0.5/c_0. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level rejection rule. Default: 0.05. used computation, API consistency. Pass value predictable_rejections() evaluating rejection. gammas Numeric vector NULL. Predictable centering sequence. NULL, constructed lagged running mean. clip_max Numeric. Maximum e-process value. Default: 1e7. strict Logical. TRUE, violation bound condition time point stop execution error. FALSE (default), warning issued e-process still computed. Note violations invalidate e-process guarantee, strict = TRUE recommended formal inference.

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
text: data.frame columns: t, e_pq, e_qp, log_e_pq, log_e_qp, c_seq, lambda_used

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
text: e-process computed : $$\log E_t(\lambda) = \sum_{=1}^t \Bigl[\lambda\,\hat{\delta}_i         - \psi_{E,c_i}(\lambda)\,(\hat{\delta}_i - \gamma_i)^2\Bigr]$$ $$\psi_{E,c}(\lambda) = \frac{-\log(1 - c\lambda) - c\lambda}{c^2}$$ evaluated step current \(c_i\). LAMBDA CHOICE: lambda = 0.5/c_0 conservative default stays well within valid domain [0, 1/c_0). better power, lambda can tuned expected signal size, must never reach 1/c_0. VALIDITY CHECK: function verifies |hat_delta_i| <= c_i/2 step warns violated. Violations invalidate e-process guarantee.

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable
text: bound sequence c_seq (centering sequence gammas) must predictable: c_i fixed known time - 1, scores1[]/scores2[] (hence hat_delta_i) observed — formally, \(c_i\) \(\mathcal{F}_{-1}\)-measurable. bound chosen seeing hat_delta_i (e.g. derived realised data range) invalidates e-process guarantee, even numerically satisfies |hat_delta_i| <= c_i/2.

title: Fixed-lambda e-process with predictable bounds (Proposition 7) — eprocess_predictable

what: Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
title: Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
text: Determine rejection times e-process output

title: Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
text: ep data.frame. Output eprocess(). alpha Numeric. Significance level. Threshold 2/alpha.

title: Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
text: Named list elements: threshold — rejection threshold (2 / alpha). tau_pq — first t e_pq >= threshold (NA never crossed). tau_qp — first t e_qp >= threshold (NA never crossed). reject_pq — logical: \(H_0^w(p,q)\) ever rejected? reject_qp — logical: \(H_0^w(q,p)\) ever rejected?

title: Determine rejection times for an e-process output — eprocess_rejections
code: scores1 <- c(-0.04, -0.09, -0.01, -0.16) scores2 <- c(-0.09, -0.16, -0.04, -0.25) ep <- eprocess(scores1, scores2, alpha = 0.05) eprocess_rejections(ep, alpha = 0.05) #> $threshold #> [1] 40 #>  #> $tau_pq #> [1] NA #>  #> $tau_qp #> [1] NA #>  #> $reject_pq #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $reject_qp #> [1] FALSE #>

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
text: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
code: ge_boundary(v, alpha, rho, c, s_lo = -10, s_hi = 500)

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
text: v Numeric vector >= 0. Intrinsic time values. alpha Numeric (0,1). ONE-SIDED significance level. rho Numeric > 0. rho_from_vopt(). c Numeric > 0. Sub-exponential scale. s_lo Numeric. Lower search bound uniroot. Default: -10. s_hi Numeric. Upper search bound uniroot. Default: 500.

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
text: Numeric vector boundary values (cumulative-sum scale).

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
text: Computes \(u_{GE}(v; \alpha, \rho, c) = \sup\{s : m(s,v) < 1/\alpha\}\) solving m(s, v) = 1/alpha numerically s via uniroot(), separately v_i. Root-finding fallback: search starts [s_lo, s_hi]; m(s_hi, v_i) yet crossed target, s_hi doubled retried. still fails, warning issued s_hi returned conservative fallback value. Increase s_hi directly warning appears often (e.g. large v small alpha); increase abs(s_lo) root found small v. Computed elementwise; can slow long vectors — consider caching boundary values (alpha, rho, c) reused.

title: Gamma-exponential mixture boundary — ge_boundary
code: rho <- rho_from_vopt(v_opt = 10, alpha = 0.025) ge_boundary(v = 1:3, alpha = 0.025, rho = rho, c = 2) #> [1] 10.12051 11.43416 12.49748

Probability floor used taking logarithms. Default 1e-15. Set 0 disable clipping.

title: Logarithmic score for binary and categorical forecasts — log_score

title: Logarithmic score for binary and categorical forecasts — log_score
text: logarithmic score unbounded . used directly finite-sample bounded-difference confidence sequences e-processes. binary outcomes, use winkler_score() winkler_cs() Winkler construction appropriate. unbounded score differences, use cs_asymptotic() supply genuine predictable bounds eprocess_predictable().

title: Logarithmic score for binary and categorical forecasts — log_score

title: Summarise predictable bounds e-process — predictable_rejections

title: Summarise predictable bounds e-process — predictable_rejections
code: scores1 <- c(0.10, 0.20, 0.15, 0.25) scores2 <- c(0.05, 0.10, 0.10, 0.20) c_seq <- rep(1, length(scores1)) ep <- eprocess_predictable(scores1, scores2, c_seq = c_seq) predictable_rejections(ep, alpha = 0.05) #> $threshold #> [1] 40 #>  #> $tau_pq #> [1] NA #>  #> $tau_qp #> [1] NA #>  #> $reject_pq #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $reject_qp #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $c_range #> [1] 1 1 #>  #> $lambda #> [1] 0.5 #>

what: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
text: Computes positively oriented (negated) QLIKE quasi-likelihood loss variance forecasts.

title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
text: sigma2_hat Numeric vector. Forecast variance (strictly positive). sigma2 Numeric vector. Realised variance (strictly positive).

title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
text: Numeric vector negated QLIKE scores. Higher better. Maximum value 0, achieved perfect forecast sigma2_hat = sigma2. Unbounded .

title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
text: Standard QLIKE loss $$L_{QL}(\hat\sigma^2, \sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\hat\sigma^2} -   \log\frac{\sigma^2}{\hat\sigma^2} - 1.$$ loss-oriented (lower = better, minimum 0 perfect forecast), function negates : \(S_{QL} = -L_{QL}\). Literature note: sources define QLIKE log(sigma2_hat) + sigma2 / sigma2_hat, differs constants form . loss normalised minimum 0 negated positive orientation.

title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score
text: QLIKE unbounded . used directly finite-sample bounded-difference confidence sequences e-processes. Use cs_asymptotic() QLIKE-based confidence sequences, use eprocess_predictable() genuine ex ante predictable bounds available. QLIKE compatible Winkler construction Winkler scores restricted binary outcomes probability forecasts.

title: Negated QLIKE score for variance forecasts — qlike_score

what: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
title: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
text: Maps user-specified intrinsic time v_opt (point boundary tightest) rho tuning parameter, via Lambert W formula Howard et al. (2021), Proposition 3 (paper-exact).

title: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
text: v_opt Numeric > 0. Intrinsic time boundary tightest. Recommended default CR23: 10. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level (one-sided). two-sided boundary level alpha, pass alpha/2 .

title: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
text: Numeric > 0. rho tuning parameter.

title: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt
text: $$\rho = \frac{v_{opt}}{-W_{-1}(-\alpha^2 / e) - 1}$$ lower branch \(W_{-1}\) defined x [-1/e, 0) returns values <= -1. alpha (0, 1), -alpha^2/e always (-1/e, 0), branch well-defined.

title: Convert optimal intrinsic time to rho — rho_from_vopt

what: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
text: Returns lo, hi derived scale parameters c_thm1, c_thm23 score difference process hat_delta_t = S(p, y) - S(q, y), cases genuine, theorem-valid bound available.

title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
text: scoring_rule Character. One : "brier", "spherical" — bounded, exact finite-sample c. "winkler" — descriptive helper one-sided CS log score. "tick" — unbounded; returns NULL guidance. "crps", "crps_normal", "crps_empirical", "crps_std" — unbounded; returns NULL guidance. "log", "qlike" — unbounded; returns NULL guidance.

title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
text: Named list elements lo, hi, c_thm1, c_thm23 bounded rules, NULL unbounded rules (informative message).

title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
text: Convention (utils.R::score_diff_scales): c_thm1  = (hi - lo) / 2   # Theorem 1:      |delta_i| <= c c_thm23 = hi - lo        # Theorems 2 & 3: |delta_i| <= c/2

title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds
text: Brier / Spherical — individual scores lie [-1, 0] (Brier) [0, 1] (Spherical), score differences lie [-1, 1] either way. bound exact yields finite-sample anytime-valid CS via Hoeffding/Bernstein. Winkler — bounded 1; lower bound problem-dependent, lo = -Inf hi = 1 used, descriptive helper one-sided CS wrapper winkler_cs(). intended generic Hoeffding/Bernstein use (Theorem 1 requires finite symmetric interval). Tick loss — unbounded general financial returns. bound derived empirical data range ex-post filtration-respecting, justify finite-sample anytime validity. Use cs_asymptotic() tick comparisons. CRPS (normal, t, empirical) — unbounded, since predictive distributions realised outcomes unbounded. historical data range ex-post surrogate provide theorem-valid c Hoeffding/Bernstein. Use cs_asymptotic(), supply genuine ex ante bounds problem-specific code available. Log / QLIKE — unbounded. binary log-score comparisons, use winkler_score() + winkler_cs() Winkler construction appropriate. categorical log-score, QLIKE, unbounded score differences, use cs_asymptotic(), eprocess_predictable() genuine ex ante predictable bounds.

title: Score difference bounds for a named scoring rule — score_bounds

what: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
title: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
text: Given score-difference bounds [lo, hi], computes two scale constants used elsewhere seqcomp: c_thm1  = (hi - lo) / 2 — sub-Gaussian scale Theorem 1, |delta_i| <= c_thm1. c_thm23 = hi - lo — sub-exponential scale Theorems 2 & 3, |delta_i| <= c_thm23 / 2.

title: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
text: lo Numeric. Lower bound score difference (usually - b). hi Numeric. Upper bound score difference (usually b - ).

title: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
text: Named list elements c_thm1 c_thm23.

title: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales
text: conventions bound quantity: centering, delta_i lies [-(hi-lo)/2, (hi-lo)/2], max|delta_i| = (hi-lo)/2, equals c_thm1 c_thm23 / 2.

title: Score difference bounds -> sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential scale — score_diff_scales

what: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: seqcomp provides tools comparing probabilistic forecasters sequentially, following anytime-valid framework Choe Ramdas (2024).

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: package built around score difference $$\hat{\delta}_t = S(p_t, y_t) - S(q_t, y_t),$$ scores positively oriented, larger values better. Positive score differences favour forecaster p; negative score differences favour forecaster q.

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: applications, start compare_forecasts(). computes pointwise scores, running mean score differences, confidence sequences, e-processes one call.

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: package includes positively oriented scoring rules brier_score(), log_score(), spherical_score(), tick_loss(), qlike_score(), winkler_score(), crps_normal(), crps_empirical(), crps_std().

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: Use cs_hoeffding() Hoeffding-style confidence sequences, cs_bernstein() empirical Bernstein confidence sequences, cs_asymptotic() asymptotic confidence sequences finite-sample boundedness available.

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: Use eprocess() main sub-exponential mixture e-process eprocess_rejections() extract first rejection times. multi-step forecasts, see eprocess_lag(). predictable time-varying bounds, see eprocess_predictable().

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: binary probability forecasts unbounded base scores, use winkler_score(), winkler_cs(), winkler_etest(), winkler_compare().

title: seqcomp: Sequential Comparison of Probabilistic Forecasts — seqcomp-package
text: Choe, Y. J. Ramdas, . (2024). Comparing Sequential Forecasters. Operations Research, 72(4), 1368-1387. Howard, S. R., Ramdas, ., McAuliffe, J. Sekhon, J. (2021). Time-uniform, nonparametric, nonasymptotic confidence sequences. Annals Statistics, 49(2).

what: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
title: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
text: Computes positively oriented spherical score. Vector probability input treated binary; matrix probability input treated categorical.

title: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
text: p Numeric vector [0, 1] binary forecasts, numeric matrix whose rows probability vectors categorical forecasts. y binary vector input, numeric vector {0, 1}. categorical matrix input, integer vector {1, ..., K}, K = ncol(p).

title: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
text: Numeric vector scores [0, 1]. Higher better.

title: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score
text: binary forecasts, computes $$S(p, y) = \frac{py + (1-p)(1-y)}{\sqrt{p^2 + (1-p)^2}}.$$ categorical forecasts, computes $$S(\mathbf{p}, y) = \frac{p_y}{\|\mathbf{p}\|_2},$$ p_y forecast probability assigned realised category. Score differences lie [-1, 1], use c = 1 Theorem 1 c = 2 Theorems 2 3.

title: Spherical score for binary and categorical forecasts — spherical_score

what: Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
title: Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
text: Computes positively oriented (negated) tick/quantile loss (Koenker & Bassett, 1978).

title: Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
text: q Numeric vector. Quantile forecasts level alpha. y Numeric vector. Realised outcomes. alpha Numeric (0,1). Quantile level.

title: Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
text: Numeric vector negated tick loss scores. Higher = better.

title: Negated tick loss for quantile forecasts — tick_loss
text: standard tick loss $$\rho_\alpha(u) = u \left(\alpha - \mathbb{1}(u < 0)\right),$$ \(u = y - q_\alpha\) forecast error. loss-oriented (lower = better), function negates : $$S_T(q, y; \alpha) = -(y - q)\left(\alpha - \mathbb{1}(y < q)\right).$$ Tick loss unbounded general real-valued outcomes. Bounds derived empirical data range ex-post provide theorem-valid constants finite-sample Hoeffding/Bernstein confidence sequences e-processes. Sign convention: negation means hat_delta_t > 0 forecaster p smaller tick loss, hence better quantile forecast, forecaster q.

title: Full Winkler comparison pipeline (Proposition 4) — winkler_compare
text: p Numeric vector (0,1). q Numeric vector (0,1). y Numeric vector containing 0 1. Binary outcomes. alpha Numeric (0,1). Default: 0.05. base_score Function. Default: log_score. v_opt Numeric > 0. Default: 10. lower_bound Numeric NULL. See winkler_cs().

title: Full Winkler comparison pipeline (Proposition 4) — winkler_compare
text: Named list elements: winkler_scores — raw Winkler score vector. cs — data.frame winkler_cs(). etest_p_worse — one-sided e-process testing whether p worse q. etest_q_worse — one-sided e-process testing whether q worse p. rejections — list one-sided rejection summaries.

what: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
text: Applies Winkler normalisation constructs one-sided upper confidence sequence mean Winkler score W_t = (1/t)*sum w_i. CS takes form (-Inf, U_t], valid uniformly t >= 1.

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
code: winkler_cs(   p,   q,   y,   alpha = 0.05,   base_score = log_score,   v_opt = 10,   lower_bound = NULL )

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
text: p Numeric vector (0,1). Forecasts model 1. q Numeric vector (0,1). Forecasts model 2. y Numeric vector containing 0 1. Binary outcomes. alpha Numeric (0,1). Significance level. Default: 0.05. base_score Function. Underlying scoring rule. Default: log_score. v_opt Numeric > 0. Optimal intrinsic time. Default: 10. lower_bound Numeric NULL. Analytical lower bound w_i two-sided CS via Corollary 2. NULL (default), returns one-sided CS . supplied, must satisfy w_i >= lower_bound almost surely.

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
text: data.frame columns t, estimate, lower, upper. lower = -Inf always (one-sided) unless lower_bound supplied.

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
text: Scale convention: Winkler score bounded 1, c/2 = 1, c = 2. hardcoded — change c without re-deriving bound.

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs
text: U_t < 0 t, time-uniform evidence forecaster 1 (p) worse forecaster 2 (q) average — .e. rejection evidence p, . generally, W_t > 0 suggests p outperforms q; W_t < 0 suggests q outperforms p.

title: One-sided empirical Bernstein CS for Winkler scores (Proposition 4) — winkler_cs

Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
text: Normalises score difference S(p,y) - S(q,y) maximum possible score difference given forecaster ordering, mapping result (-Inf, 1] (Proposition 4, Choe & Ramdas 2023). Used apply Theorems 2 & 3 unbounded scoring rules binary outcomes.

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
code: winkler_score(p, q, y, base_score = log_score, eps = 1e-08)

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
text: p Numeric vector (0,1). Forecasts model 1. q Numeric vector (0,1). Forecasts model 2. y Numeric vector containing 0 1. Binary outcomes. base_score Function. underlying scoring rule S(p, y). Must accept two arguments: forecast probability outcome. Default: log_score (eps clipping). eps Numeric. Zero-protection normaliser denominator. Default: 1e-8 (matches Python comparecast convention).

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
text: Numeric vector. Winkler scores (-Inf, 1]. Upper bound 1 tight: w = 1 y = 1(p > q).

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
text: $$w(p, q, y) = \frac{S(p,y) - S(q,y)}{S(p, \mathbb{1}(p>q)) - S(q, \mathbb{1}(p>q))}$$ convention 0/0 := 0. lower bound problem-dependent (depends extreme p q can ). two-sided CS via Corollary 2, user must establish finite lower bound analytically. finite lower bound can guaranteed, use one-sided (upper) CS , CR23 MLB experiments.

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
text: Strictly limited binary outcomes y {0, 1} probability forecasts p, q (0, 1). applicable QLIKE continuous-outcome scoring rules. See CR23 Section G discussion. use Theorems 2 & 3: upper bound = 1 implies c/2 = 1, use c = 2 GE boundary e-process calls.

title: Winkler-normalized binary score — winkler_score
